Makepeace Secrets Exposed!
Q&A Session 1
Module 	6

-- 1 of 46 --

I 	would 	like 	to 	create 	a 	tiered
package 	for 	new 	clients 	in 	a 	specific
niche market.
I'm 	picturing 	it 	as 	a 	kind 	of 	"menu"
-giving 	different 	choices 	to 	new
clients 	who 	are 	just 	starting 	out 	so
they 	can 	get 	the 	services 	they 	want,
and 	not 	pay 	for 	what 	they 	don't 	need.
Where 	I'm 	stuck 	at 	is 	knowing 	what
should 	go 	in 	the 	"ala 	carte" 	the
"entree" 	and 	the 	"full 	meal."
If 	I'm 	offering 	Online 	Copywriting
Services 	- 	such 	as 	sales 	copy, 	auto
responder emails, and ebook
writing...can 	you 	give 	some 	concrete
examples 	of 	what 	specific 	services
could 	go 	into 	each 	package 	I 	offer?
-- Cheryl

-- 2 of 46 --

Two Questions in Search of Answers:
1. What message does your tiered fee
structure send to prospective clients?
2. How does it facilitate closing the deal?

-- 3 of 46 --

SOLUTION: KISS.
Create a rate card
-- but keep it 	to yourself!
FIRST, decide how much money you need to make each
month, then double it and divide by 160. That’s your
hourly rate.
SECOND, identify the kinds of projects you want to work
on.
THIRD, calculate the amount of time each of these
projects typically takes you. Divide by the number of
pages to arrive at your page rate.
FOURTH, when asked what you charge for a particular
type of job, ask, “Who does the research?”
If the answer is, “You do,” calculate how many hours
researching the project will take. Add a 20% contingency.
Multiply times your hourly rate.

-- 4 of 46 --

FOURTH, ask “How many pages to you see this being?”
Double the client’s page estimate as a contingency in case
the page count increases or the client requires multiple
drafts.
FIFTH, multiply your page rate by the number of pages
including your contingency. Add the amount you’re
charging to research the project. Say, “I get $X for this kind
of job.”
SIXTH, when the client feigns shock and disbelief at your
exorbitant rate, be magnanimous ...
A) Offer to do it for less, but with adjustments to your
invoice if the page count increases or if more than
three drafts are required.
B) Offer to do it for less if the client provides the
research.
C) Offer to do it for less, but with a royalty (up to $50/M
in the mail or 10% of sales, whichever is more).
D) Ask the client what other projects he has cooking.
Offer him a package deal with a discount if he retains
you for several of these other projects.

-- 5 of 46 --

AND SEVENTH, FORMALIZE 	your agreement
in writing to avoid misunderstandings when
it’s time for the client to pay your invoice.

-- 6 of 46 --

I'm now working on a couple of
courses and reading the classics.
I haven't selected a niche
yet, although I've got a couple
in mind.
How will I know to begin
seeking jobs? Is it just a
feeling that I'll have, knowing
that I'm ready?
-- Robert

-- 7 of 46 --

1. Select your niche.
A. What are you passionate about?
B. What do you already know a lot about
or would love to learn more about?
C. Which niches are making other
writers richest?
2. Study what’s being done in that niche
now. Become your prospect.
3. Begin creating promotions for real
products in that niche
4. Show them to prospective clients.
5. Call prospective clients with ideas.
Your prospects and clients
will let you know when you’re ready.

-- 8 of 46 --

I'm 	an 	ultra-competitive 	person 	who
hates 	to 	lose 	at 	anything.
I'll 	easily 	put 	40-50 	hours 	on 	a
client 	project, 	trying 	to 	get 	the 	sales
letter 	perfect 	for 	my 	client.
I'll 	pay 	another 	copywriter 	to
critique 	it 	for 	me 	before 	I 	ever 	turn
it 	over 	to 	my 	client, 	just 	to 	make 	sure
the 	copy 	is 	rock-solid.
Yet, 	I 	still 	don't 	produce 	a 	big 	hit
for 	my 	clients 	with 	any 	consistency...
which 	frustrates 	the 	hell 	out 	of 	me.
What 	am 	I 	doing 	wrong? 	My 	ego 	is
taking 	a 	serious 	beating.
-- 	Mike
Question #3

-- 9 of 46 --

13 Reasons Why Promos Fail:
1. The product was ill-conceived.
2. The pricing was too low or too high.
3. The guarantee was weak.
4. The premiums stunk.
5. The list was poorly chosen.
6. The promo was not delivered well.
7. Major news events or prospects’ life
events depressed readership rates.

-- 10 of 46 --

13 Reasons Why Promos Fail:
8. The theme for the main headline and/or
opening copy failed to resonate.
9. The promised benefits were not credible.
10. The closing did not convince prospects
of the need to order now (urgency).
11. The order device was difficult to use.
12. There was a technical disruption in
order flow.
13. The copy sucked.

-- 11 of 46 --

Three kinds of flops:
1) Low response rate but high average sale:
Analysis – not many read the copy, but those
who did were sold in spades.
Culprit: Headline and opening copy.
Solution: Test several new headline/opening
combinations.

-- 12 of 46 --

Three kinds of flops:
2) Good response but low average sale:
Analysis – the headline and opening copy are
working, but the sales copy itself isn’t as
convincing as it should be.
Culprits: Product, price, premiums, offer, weak
benefit copy, weak credibility devices.
Solution: Identify weakest areas and rewrite
affected sections.

-- 13 of 46 --

Three kinds of flops:
3) Low response and average sale: Analysis –
nothing worked.
Culprits: All of the above.
Solution: Rethink everything.

-- 14 of 46 --

I 	am 	reading 	this 	month's 	issue 	of 	The
Screaming Eagle.
How 	do 	you 	apply 	this 	process 	if 	you 	are
writing 	a 	direct 	mail 	piece 	to, 	say,
Commercial 	Realtors 	-- 	from 	a 	Commercial
Mortgage 	Broker 	who 	is 	interested 	in
partnering 	with 	them 	to 	close 	their
commercial real estate deals?
In 	this 	case, 	their 	buying 	habits 	do 	not
come 	in 	play. 	It 	seems 	like 	you 	are 	limited
on research.
Yes, 	you 	know 	the 	Commercial 	Realtor
wants 	to 	close 	more 	of 	his 	deals, 	not 	lose
deals 	because 	of 	financing 	issues.
I 	guess 	I 	want 	to 	know 	how 	I 	can 	follow
this 	process 	you 	describe 	in 	a 	scenario
like this?
-- 	Jay

-- 15 of 46 --

I've 	never 	seen 	a 	good 	answer 	to
this 	question 	in 	any 	direct-marketing
text 	or 	course. 	It's 	this:
If 	you're 	mailing 	promotions 	to 	a
fixed 	audience 	(e.g. 	investors) 	how
often 	do 	you 	mail 	them, 	and 	when/how 	do
you 	change 	your 	promotion?
For 	example, 	if 	you 	had 	a 	universe
of 	one 	million 	names, 	once 	you've
mailed 	them 	your 	control, 	then 	what?
A 	new 	package 	next 	month, 	or 	three
months 	later? 	Or 	a 	remail 	of 	the
original 	package 	6 	months 	later?
I've 	never 	seen 	anyone 	talk 	about
this 	in 	depth!
-- 	James

-- 16 of 46 --



-- 17 of 46 --



-- 18 of 46 --



-- 19 of 46 --



-- 20 of 46 --

My 	client 	is 	launching 	a 	new
financial 	trading 	product 	in 	the 	UK.
Would 	you 	put 	together 	a 	complete
selling 	message 	(i.e. 	covering 	every
possible 	benefit 	whilst 	plucking 	every
possible emotional banjo 	string)?
Or 	would 	you 	narrow 	the 	focus 	on 	a
couple 	of 	key 	things 	for 	the 	first
launch 	message... 	and 	then 	create 	an
extended 	launch 	period 	with 	several
messages 	that 	each 	focus 	on 	a 	different
key distinction.
Said 	another 	way: 	To 	do 	a 	product
launch, 	should 	I 	go 	for 	the 	jugular, 	or
give 	the 	prospect 	time 	to 	let 	his 	guard
down 	first 	by 	a 	softer 	graduated
introduction?
-- Nathan

-- 21 of 46 --



-- 22 of 46 --



-- 23 of 46 --

Can you talk about handling
drop-offs when a product or
service is delivered over a
one-year period?
We have - what is for us - a
big back-end. ($995) To
encourage a better percentage
of our clients to purchase this
product, we've chosen to offer
it over a 12 month period.
I am aware of potential
drop-offs at certain points and
I am wonder if you may have any
suggestions on how to maximize
retention.
-- Phillip

-- 24 of 46 --

Four Ways to Keep Continuity Customers
Much, Much Longer
1. Offer price/premium incentives to those who
pay cash up front.
2. Offer continuity customers the opportunity to
convert to annual once per quarter.
3. Over‐deliver with unadvertised surprise
bonuses.
4. Tease each month’s benefits in advance.
5. Offer a big bonus at the end of each year.

-- 25 of 46 --

Any tips for increasing
conversion on name squeeze
pages?
-- Chris

-- 26 of 46 --

Squeeze 	Page 	Fundamentals
1. Keep ‘em SHORT.
2. Confirm what they’ll get.
3. Establish your credibility.
4. Demonstrate your privacy policy.
5. Demonstrate your cancellation policy.
6. Show your business credentials.
7. Do what you say you’ll do.
8. Add a viral element.
9. Consider adding a forced free trial.

-- 27 of 46 --

Do you recommend any German
Copywriters?
Are there any?
-- Robert

-- 28 of 46 --

What positioning tactics
would you recommend for
copywriters to take right
now?
Should a copywriter
change his positioning to
better reflect the looming
recession?
-- Harun

-- 29 of 46 --

Many of my clients are not
willing to spend the money on
bigger ads in the paper or to
try to have the ads look more
like editorials than actual
ads.
How can I get clients that
have the money to advertise in
other markets ... to test ...
and try direct mail?
-- Lawton

-- 30 of 46 --

What is the most effective
way to track adverts in the
paper and online?
I see a little code but
how does that help the
advertiser?
-- Lawton

-- 31 of 46 --



-- 32 of 46 --

In 	the 	past, 	corporate 	retrenching
has 	led 	discharged 	employees 	to 	pursue
new 	career 	paths 	- 	with 	less 	dependency
on corporate jobs.
1. 	Do 	you 	see 	this 	happening 	again,
now?
2. 	If 	so, 	do 	you 	think 	these 	newly
minted 	entrepreneurs 	are 	a 	good 	market
for 	Info 	Products; 	i.e.: 	business
creation 	advice, 	as 	well 	as 	market
specific 	advice 	or 	"how 	to's" 	and,
3. 	What 	markets, 	categories 	or 	other
areas 	do 	you 	believe 	will 	yield 	the
greatest 	returns 	(for 	info 	marketers)
i.e.: 	Where's 	the 	hungriest 	crowd?
4. 	Is 	there 	any 	special 	or 	unique 	way
you 	would 	suggest 	to 	connect 	with 	these
new or potential entrepreneurs?
-- Mark

-- 33 of 46 --

Regarding Customer Acquisition:
Is there a place for "Giving away
for free" front ends in order to
acquire a new name?
Or would you always charge for
a front end to cover immediate
costs?
Would it be correct to say, you
can loose a percentage on the
first front end to get the name as
long as the new name buys within a
reasonable period? And so
breaking-even 60 - 90 days later?
-- Phillip

-- 34 of 46 --

When to Consider Multiple-Step
Offline Sales Campaigns:
1. When the product price is enormous.
2. When you need to cull prospects from a huge
universe cheaply before presenting your full
sales message.
3. When a live salesperson is required to answer
questions and address objections.
4. When closing the sale requires more than a
simple order form – as with loan applications.
5. When the product is customizable.

-- 35 of 46 --

Times to Consider Multiple-Step
ONline Sales Campaigns:
1. Always.

-- 36 of 46 --

When it comes to a
premium/offer summary page,
the sort of thing we put in
an acquisition promo right
before the response
device...
...what are your thoughts
on the pros and cons of
showing just your best offer
vs. say, all 3 options?
And do you have any data
to support which method
works best?
-- Andrew
Question #18

-- 37 of 46 --



-- 38 of 46 --

How can I improve my process
for creating information
products?
-- Phillip

-- 39 of 46 --

7 Key Questions to Answer:
1. Who’s your best prospect?
2. What’s the most cost-efficient way to reach
him?
3. What’s his most pressing problem or desire
now?
4. What can you deliver that will solve that
problem or fulfill that desire?
5. What product format has your prospect
shown he’s most willing to accept?
6. Can you achieve your objective in a single
installment or would a series be more
effective?
7. What is he willing to pay?

-- 40 of 46 --

In slow economic times,
people are going to be
looking for more ways to
make money.
What are the key
psychological triggers you
hit in down economic times?
-- Matt

-- 41 of 46 --

Four Dominant Emotions
in Tough Times
1. Fear
2. Helplessness
3. Resentment/Anger
4. Desire for security

-- 42 of 46 --

1. Would it be better to position
oneself in B2C over B2B markets right now?
My logic says B2B markets take a harder
beating with a looming downturn of the
economy.
2. I live in Germany and plan to move
to Australia with wife and daughter in
about 2 - 3 years from now.
What would you do: Go into English
speaking markets as a copywriter right now
(while in Germany), or stay in the German
& Swiss market, and then work for German
companies from Australia? It seems the
Australian copywriters write more for the
USA anyway.
3. Are we going towards times where
long sales letters with lots of non-hype
information and valuable background are
going to pull best? 	Especially outside
the newsletter world, for example in the
venture capital/private equity world?
-- Markus

-- 43 of 46 --

1) Can you point me to some good
resources on positioning? (books,
courses, etc.)
2) If you were a newbie copywriter
in today's market...what letter
would you send out to get people
interested? (A sample would be
appreciated.)
3) What do you do when you get
stuck or hit a wall?
4) How many times do you edit your
work?
5) Who are your mentors (now and
in the past)?
-- Steve

-- 44 of 46 --

Damn, 	Clayton. 	You're 	making 	me
feel smart.
Last 	month 	I 	was 	evaluating 	my
business, 	making 	plans 	for 	2008. 	I
knew 	that 	I 	had 	to 	change 	my 	model 	and
become 	something 	of 	a 	"virtual 	VP 	of
Marketing" to small companies.
You've 	confirmed 	this. 	I've 	got 	the
skills 	and 	knowledge 	to 	make 	it 	work.
What 	I 	lack 	is 	a 	clear 	plan 	of 	attack.
How 	do 	I 	find 	these 	companies?
How 	do 	I 	approach 	them?
What 	do 	I 	say... 	send 	them... 	or
offer?
I'm 	willing 	to 	do 	the 	work 	to 	make
it 	happen. 	I'm 	just 	not 	sure 	WHAT 	to
do.
-- Sid

-- 45 of 46 --

Any More 	QUESTIONS?

-- 46 of 46 --
